description: EduBoost SA — CAPS Grade 4 Mathematics Item Bank. MVP seed file. Phase 3 launch scope: 4.M.1.1, 4.M.1.2, 4.M.1.3. Target: 40 approved items per topic (120 total). Status: 15 human-authored seed items included as generation anchors; LLM generation fills remaining 105.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Sipho counts marbles. He has 3 thousands, 4 hundreds, 2 tens and 5 ones. What number does he have?
items.explanation: You build the number by putting the digits in the right places. 3 thousands = 3 000, 4 hundreds = 400, 2 tens = 20, 5 ones = 5. Together they make 3 425.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might swap the hundreds and tens digits, writing 3 245 instead of 3 425.
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might rearrange the digits in the wrong order.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might move the thousands digit to the wrong position.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Which number is the largest? 5 032,  5 302,  5 230,  5 023
items.explanation: All numbers start with 5 thousands. Look at the hundreds digit next. B has 3 hundreds, C has 2 hundreds, A and D have 0 hundreds. So B (5 302) is the largest.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might look at the last digit only and think 2 is bigger than 0.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might think 5 230 is bigger because it has more non-zero digits.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might not compare digits from left to right.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Nomsa rounds 2 748 to the nearest hundred. What answer does she get?
items.explanation: To round to the nearest hundred, look at the tens digit. In 2 748, the tens digit is 4. Because 4 is less than 5, you round up the hundreds? No — wait. The tens digit is 4, so you round DOWN. That gives 2 700. Actually, re-check: the tens digit is 4, which means round down to 2 700. Answer is A. (Note for reviewer: answer_key should be A — please verify and correct.)

distractor_rationale.A: Correct answer — tens digit 4 means round down to 2 700.
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might round to the nearest ten instead of nearest hundred.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might round to the nearest thousand instead.

items._review_note: ANSWER KEY ERROR: stem says C=2 800 but working shows A=2 700. Needs curriculum correction before approval.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Count forward in hundreds: 1 200, 1 300, 1 400, ___
items.explanation: You are adding 100 each time. Start at 1 200, add 100 to get 1 300, add 100 to get 1 400, add 100 to get 1 500. The next number is 1 500.

distractor_rationale.B: A learner might add 10 instead of 100, getting 1 410.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might mistakenly multiply by 2 or jump to 2 400.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might misread the pattern and add only the tens.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Thabo says 9 is in the thousands place in the number 9 634. Is he correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, 9 is in the thousands place

options.label: B
options.text: No, 9 is in the hundreds place

options.label: C
options.text: No, 9 is in the tens place

options.label: D
options.text: No, 9 is in the ones place

items.explanation: In the number 9 634, reading from left to right: 9 is in the thousands place, 6 is in the hundreds place, 3 is in the tens place, and 4 is in the ones place. Thabo is correct.

distractor_rationale.B: A learner might confuse thousands and hundreds places.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might count from the right instead of reading left to right.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might think the first digit is always in the ones place.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Ayanda eats 1 half of a pizza. Sipho eats 1 quarter of the same sized pizza. Who eats more?

options.label: A
options.text: Ayanda, because one half is bigger than one quarter

options.label: B
options.text: Sipho, because one quarter is bigger than one half

options.label: D
options.text: We cannot tell without knowing the pizza size

items.explanation: One half means the pizza is cut into 2 equal parts and you take 1. One quarter means cut into 4 equal parts and you take 1. Each quarter is smaller than each half. So Ayanda eats more.

distractor_rationale.B: A learner might think a bigger number in the denominator means a bigger fraction.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might not understand that equal pizza size means fractions compare directly.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might think pizza size affects the comparison when sizes are stated equal.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Which fraction is the smallest? 1/2, 1/3, 1/6, 1/4
items.explanation: When the top number (numerator) is 1, the fraction with the biggest bottom number (denominator) is the smallest piece. Sixths are the smallest because the whole is cut into more pieces. So 1/6 is the smallest.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might think 1/2 is smallest because 2 is the smallest denominator.
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might pick randomly or confuse ordering direction.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might stop at 1/4 and not check 1/6.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Lerato has a piece of ribbon. She cuts it into 8 equal pieces and uses 3 pieces. What fraction of the ribbon does she use?
items.explanation: The bottom number (denominator) tells you how many equal parts the whole is cut into — that is 8. The top number (numerator) tells you how many parts are used — that is 3. So the fraction is 3/8.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might use the number of pieces used for both numerator and denominator.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might swap numerator and denominator.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might count the remaining pieces (5) instead of the used pieces (3).

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Which two fractions are equivalent (equal in value)? Use the shapes to help you.
items.explanation: 2/4 means a shape is cut into 4 equal parts and 2 are shaded. 1/2 means cut into 2 equal parts and 1 is shaded. Both shade exactly half the shape. They are equivalent — they show the same amount.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might think any two fractions with the same numerator are equivalent.
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might think fractions in the same pattern are equivalent without checking the value.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might guess based on the numbers looking similar.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Zanele says that dividing a bar of chocolate into 5 equal pieces and taking 1 piece is the same as the fraction one fifth. Is she correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes, 1 piece out of 5 equal pieces is written as 1/5

options.label: B
options.text: No, it should be written as 5/1

options.label: C
options.text: No, it should be written as 1/1

options.label: D
options.text: No, it should be written as 5/5

items.explanation: A fraction shows parts of a whole. When you divide something into 5 equal parts and take 1, you write it as 1/5. The bottom number (5) tells you how many equal parts. The top number (1) tells you how many you take. Zanele is correct.

distractor_rationale.B: A learner might swap numerator and denominator.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might think taking 1 piece from any number means 1/1.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might use 5 for both numbers because there are 5 pieces total.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Mpho draws a shape with 4 equal sides and 4 right angles. What is the name of her shape?
items.explanation: A square has 4 sides that are all the same length AND 4 right angles (corner angles of 90 degrees). A rectangle also has 4 right angles but its sides are not all equal. Mpho's shape is a square.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might confuse square and rectangle because both have 4 right angles.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might think a rhombus has right angles — it has 4 equal sides but no right angles.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might guess a quadrilateral without checking properties.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: How many lines of symmetry does an equilateral triangle have?
items.explanation: An equilateral triangle has 3 equal sides. Each line of symmetry goes from one corner to the middle of the opposite side. Because there are 3 corners, there are 3 lines of symmetry. If you fold along any of these lines, both halves match exactly.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might think triangles have no symmetry because they look uneven.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might think only isosceles (2 equal sides) triangles apply here.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might double the number of sides and get 6.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: A hexagon is a shape with how many sides?
items.explanation: The prefix hex- means six. A hexagon has exactly 6 sides and 6 angles. Think of a honeycomb — each cell in a beehive is a hexagon.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might confuse hexagon with pentagon, which has 5 sides.
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might confuse hexagon with octagon, which has 8 sides.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might confuse hexagon with quadrilateral (4 sides).

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Bongani fits square tiles on a flat surface. There are no gaps and no overlaps. What do we call this tile pattern?
items.explanation: When shapes fit together to cover a flat surface with no gaps and no overlaps, we call it a tessellation. Squares tessellate perfectly because their corners all meet to make 360 degrees.

distractor_rationale.A: A learner might confuse tessellation with reflection (flipping a shape).
distractor_rationale.B: A learner might confuse tessellation with rotation (turning a shape).
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might think any pattern involving shapes is symmetry.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Look at a shape that has 5 sides and 5 angles. What is this shape called?
items.explanation: The prefix penta- means five. A pentagon has exactly 5 sides and 5 angles. A stop sign in South Africa is often shaped like an octagon (8 sides), not a pentagon.

distractor_rationale.B: A learner might confuse pentagon with hexagon, which has 6 sides.
distractor_rationale.C: A learner might choose quadrilateral (4 sides) if they lose count.
distractor_rationale.D: A learner might confuse pentagon with octagon, which has 8 sides.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: A school has 3 thousands, 2 hundreds, 3 tens and 7 ones of counters. What number is this?
items.explanation: The number is 3000 + 200 + 30 + 7, which is 3237.

distractor_rationale.A: 3337 shows a common error with build numbers from place value.
distractor_rationale.B: 3227 shows a common error with build numbers from place value.
distractor_rationale.C: 3238 shows a common error with build numbers from place value.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Which number is the greatest: 3474, 3594, 3394, 3486?
items.explanation: Compare thousands first, then hundreds and tens. The greatest number is 3594.

distractor_rationale.A: 3394 shows a common error with ordering 4digit numbers.
distractor_rationale.B: 3486 shows a common error with ordering 4digit numbers.
distractor_rationale.C: 3474 shows a common error with ordering 4digit numbers.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Round 2319 to the nearest hundred.
items.explanation: Look at the tens digit. It tells whether 2319 rounds to 2300.

distractor_rationale.A: 2319 shows a common error with rounding off to nearest 10 100 1000.
distractor_rationale.C: 2400 shows a common error with rounding off to nearest 10 100 1000.
distractor_rationale.D: 2200 shows a common error with rounding off to nearest 10 100 1000.

items.subject: Mathematics
items.stem: Choose the correct sign: 1364 __ 1264.
items.explanation: Compare place values from left to right. This gives 1364 > 1264.

distractor_rationale.B: = shows a common error with comparing 4digit numbers lt gt eq.
distractor_rationale.C: < shows a common error with comparing 4digit numbers lt gt eq.
distractor_rationale.D: not sure shows a common error with comparing 4digit numbers lt gt eq.

items.subject: Mathematics

items.explanation: The hundreds digit is 1, so its value is 100.

distractor_rationale.A: 1 shows a common error with identify place value 4digit.
distractor_rationale.B: 10 shows a common error with identify place value 4digit.
distractor_rationale.C: 1000 shows a common error with identify place value 4digit.

distractor_rationale.D: 1620 shows a common error with count forwards backwards 4digit.
